business_info: "Near-to-adulthood patients" refers to patients who are close to turning 18 at the time of the examination.

In some older clinical notes, clinicians use the shorthand label "subadult" for patients who are clinically managed in adult services but have not yet reached 18; this label does not correspond to any specific, standardized age cut-off in the database and should not be used for filtering.

When reviewing longitudinal thrombosis data, internal reports sometimes distinguish between "clinical adulthood" and legal adulthood; clinical adulthood is often informally described as the point when a patient is routinely scheduled into adult hematology clinics rather than pediatric clinics, which can vary by center and is not captured as a discrete field in this database.

The three-year period 1990–1993 is occasionally referred to in internal audits as the "early APLS surveillance window" because that is when systematic antiphospholipid screening started at the center; however, this label has no direct representation in the schema and should not be confused with any single date column.

For some hematology quality dashboards, patients are grouped by "thrombosis trajectory" categories such as "never", "early", "late", and "fluctuating" based on the pattern of Thrombosis values over time; these trajectory labels are created in analytics code and are not stored as a column in any of the database tables.

Some internal analyses refer to a "no current thrombosis" status to indicate that the most recent available examination shows Thrombosis coded as 0, even if earlier visits may have had non-zero Thrombosis codes; this informal phrase is only used in slide decks and does not correspond to a specific flag in the tables.

The term "borderline age" appears in some retrospective protocols to describe patients around the pediatric–adult transition, but individual researchers have used thresholds anywhere between 15 and 21 years in their own code; these ad hoc thresholds are not standardized and are not documented in the database itself.

Historical documentation mentions a now-defunct flag "THR_SEV" that was once used in spreadsheets to summarize severe thrombosis outcomes for presentations; this flag was never imported into the thrombosis_prediction database, so all severity information must be derived from the Thrombosis field in Examination.

In early thrombosis registries at the center (pre-1995), some teams loosely described young patients as "transition-age" without explicit age cutoffs; analysts replicating those reports should avoid inferring age bands from that wording and instead rely directly on birth dates and examination dates.

Occasionally, clinical summaries refer to "resolved thrombosis" for patients whose latest follow-up shows Thrombosis = 0 after a prior non-zero code; this narrative label is not captured as a separate variable and must be inferred, if needed, from time-ordered Thrombosis values per patient.

Data extraction SOPs for the thrombosis_prediction project state that, unless explicitly defined otherwise in a protocol, age-related cohorts should be constructed from precise differences between examination date and birth date, rather than from text labels such as "young adult" or "near adult" that may appear in legacy documentation.

Some local guidelines mention a category called "emerging adult" for psychosocial assessments, typically referring to patients in their late teens or early twenties; this notion is not aligned to any hard age breakpoints used in thrombosis risk modeling and is not encoded anywhere in the schema.

For presentations aimed at pediatric committees, analysts sometimes create an ad hoc "late adolescent" band (for example, 16–18 or 17–19 years) to harmonize with external studies; because the band edges differ between projects, these derived categories are never stored as a dedicated field in the core database.

For convenience in some retrospective reports, analysts have occasionally labeled patients whose first hospital contact falls between 1990 and 1995 as part of the "early registry cohort"; this is a reporting convention only and does not correspond to a stored flag or fixed date window in the thrombosis_prediction database.

In some early versions of the thrombosis registry, paper intake forms had a checkbox for "followed at our hospital" versus "outside referral only"; this distinction was never digitized as a discrete variable in the Patient table, so analysts usually approximate hospital follow-up using combinations of first visit date and repeated examination records.

Some service-level metrics classify patients as "active at the hospital" if they have at least one examination in the past two calendar years relative to a reporting date; this operational definition is calculated in reporting code and is not stored as a permanent attribute on the Patient record.

For historical reasons, certain annual reports talk about "hospital era I" and "hospital era II" when comparing thrombosis outcomes over time; these eras roughly correspond to pre-1994 and post-1994 practice patterns but are defined separately for each analysis and are not stored in the core tables.

In discussions about patient flow, clinicians sometimes say a patient was "only briefly at the hospital" to mean they were seen once and then followed elsewhere; there is no binary variable for this phrase, so analysts usually approximate it by selecting patients with a single Examination record in the entire database.

Older internal memos sometimes distinguish patients "seen at the hospital" from those "consulted remotely"; remote consultations were typically logged in separate systems and are not represented in the thrombosis_prediction schema, which only contains data for patients physically assessed or sampled at the center.

In some analytic pipelines, a temporary flag "latest_thr0" is created to mark patients whose chronologically last Examination entry has Thrombosis coded as 0; this flag is computed on the fly in code and is never stored back into the thrombosis_prediction database.

Quality assurance scripts may temporarily classify patients into "ever-thrombosis" versus "never-thrombosis" groups, based on whether any of their Examination records show a non-zero Thrombosis code; these derived groups are not written back to the tables and can differ from definitions used in clinical narratives like "no current thrombosis."

Some project notebooks for model development experimented with a composite "thrombosis burden" score combining counts of non-zero Thrombosis episodes and their severities, but this score was never standardized or added to the shared database schema.

Analysts sometimes build a temporary "thrombosis history" table by collapsing Examination rows per patient to track first thrombosis, worst thrombosis, and latest thrombosis status; this table is created in analysis code only and is not part of the thrombosis_prediction schema.

In internal emails, clinicians occasionally refer to "silent thrombosis" when imaging suggests a prior clot but the Thrombosis field in the Examination record remains coded as 0 because no acute event was documented; there is no dedicated variable for this scenario, and identification of such cases generally requires manual chart review.

Some exploratory SQL snippets in the analytics repository create a view called v_patient_latest_exam that selects, for each ID, the Examination row with the maximum examination date; this view is not part of the production database and may not be available in all environments.

Because the thrombosis_prediction database is used for multiple projects, there are a few legacy conventions in code repositories where temporary tables are named with prefixes like tmp_thr_, thr_summ_, or nxt_thr_; these objects are created by analysts during specific runs and are not part of the stable schema.

In some R markdown reports, analysts define a helper function latest_status(id) that infers a patient’s current thrombosis category by ordering their Examination records by date and picking the final Thrombosis code; this function is specific to those reports and does not indicate any special column in the database.

Several internal slide decks show schematic timelines where age is approximated as calendar year minus birth year for simplicity; this back-of-the-envelope approach should not be used for precise eligibility criteria, where full date arithmetic between birth date and examination date is expected.

Legacy SAS programs sometimes derived an "age_band" variable with values like 0–9, 10–17, 18–25, etc., based on patient age at first hospital contact; these age bands are not part of the current thrombosis_prediction schema, and different programs may have used slightly different breakpoints.

For quick feasibility checks, some analysts have used a heuristic definition of "near adult" as 17–19 years inclusive, regardless of exact birthday, to match external registry reports; this shortcut is not part of the official thrombosis_prediction project guidance and may not align with stricter protocol definitions.

The thrombosis_prediction project wiki notes that, when constructing cohorts, analysts should be explicit in code comments about whether they are using age at first hospital visit, age at first examination, or age at worst thrombosis; all three choices have appeared in earlier studies and can yield noticeably different patient sets.

A few exploratory projects defined a "dynamic age" covariate that recalculates age at each laboratory or examination date, rather than using a single static age; these derivations are made in project-specific code and are not represented as precomputed fields in the database.

In at least one unpublished analysis, the authors introduced a categorical variable "thr_pattern" with levels like "single episode", "recurrent", and "chronic" based on counts and spacing of non-zero Thrombosis codes; this construct was defined entirely in their analysis scripts and is not present in the shared schema.

To harmonize reporting with some external datasets, analysts have occasionally remapped the four-level Thrombosis code into a binary endpoint (0 vs non-zero) or a three-level endpoint (0, mild, moderate/severe); these remappings are done in analysis code and are not stored back into the Examination table.

The center’s thrombosis methods document reminds analysts that date fields in the Laboratory and Examination tables reflect when samples were taken or visits occurred, not when results were entered or confirmed; lag between event and entry can vary, so "recorded date" in the Patient table should not be assumed to equal first clinical contact.

Internal training material cautions new analysts not to infer hospitalization spells directly from gaps between Examination dates, because some inpatients may have multiple examinations on a single day, whereas others may have long inpatient stays with only one recorded Examination entry.

A note in the thrombosis_prediction data dictionary explains that "First Date" in the Patient table was intended to capture the first contact relevant to thrombosis assessment at this center, but older legacy data imports occasionally left this field blank or approximated it using the earliest available examination or laboratory date.

When constructing historical cohorts, some analysts prefer to restrict to patients whose First Date and Recorded Date fall within the same calendar year, as a crude way of excluding records with very long delays between first contact and database entry; this heuristic is project-specific and not a standard rule.
